event: Early hominins inhabit East Africa, part of the cradle of humankind whose populations spread across the region that includes present-day South Sudan.
Hunter-gatherer communities occupy the Nile valley and savannas of the upper White Nile basin during the early Holocene.
Nilotic and other agro-pastoral peoples settle the region, herding cattle and cultivating grains along the rivers and wetlands of the Sudd.
Ancestral Nilotic communities, forebears of the Dinka, Nuer, and Shilluk, expand across the upper Nile floodplains.
The Shilluk Kingdom emerges along the White Nile under its founder Nyikang, becoming one of the region's most organized states.
The Azande establish powerful chiefdoms and kingdoms in the southwestern forests, expanding through conquest and assimilation.
Egypt's ruler Muhammad Ali launches the Turco-Egyptian conquest of Sudan, opening the south to outside penetration.
Turco-Egyptian expeditions push up the White Nile into the southern interior, opening trade and exploration routes.
Khartoum-based merchants intensify ivory and slave raiding into the southern lands, devastating local communities.
Samuel Baker is appointed governor of Equatoria and tasked with annexing the south and suppressing the slave trade.
Charles Gordon becomes governor of Equatoria, establishing administrative posts along the upper Nile.
The Mahdist uprising erupts in Sudan, and its forces extend influence over parts of the south.
The Anglo-Egyptian Condominium is established, placing the south under joint British-Egyptian rule administered separately from the north.
Britain implements 'Southern Policy,' administering the south as a distinct region with separate development from the Arab-Muslim north.
Formal Southern Policy measures restrict northern influence, promote English and Christianity, and treat the south as a separate sphere.
The Juba Conference brings southern leaders into a unified Sudanese framework as Britain reverses Southern Policy ahead of independence.
Southern soldiers mutiny at Torit, sparking the First Sudanese Civil War (the Anyanya rebellion) before Sudan's independence.
Sudan becomes independent, with the south incorporated into the new state amid fears of northern domination.
The Addis Ababa Agreement ends the First Civil War, granting the south regional autonomy.
President Nimeiry imposes Sharia law and dissolves southern autonomy, igniting the Second Sudanese Civil War led by John Garang's SPLA/SPLM.
Omar al-Bashir seizes power in Khartoum, intensifying the war against the southern rebellion.
An SPLA split and the Bor massacre deepen internal Nuer-Dinka conflict during the wider civil war.
Famine and continued fighting devastate the south as the war drags on through the 1990s.
The Machakos Protocol grants the south the right to a future self-determination referendum, paving the way for peace talks.
The Comprehensive Peace Agreement ends the Second Civil War, creating an autonomous Government of Southern Sudan; John Garang dies in a helicopter crash months later.
Southern Sudan holds its first general elections under the autonomy arrangement, with Salva Kiir confirmed as president of the region.
South Sudanese vote overwhelmingly for independence in a January referendum, and South Sudan becomes an independent nation on 9 July with Juba as its capital.
South Sudan joins the United Nations on 14 July and is admitted to the African Union, becoming the world's newest state.
Disputes with Sudan over the oil-rich Abyei region and pipeline fees lead South Sudan to shut down oil production and brief border clashes at Heglig.
A political rupture between President Salva Kiir and Vice President Riek Machar erupts into civil war along largely ethnic Dinka-Nuer lines.
Mass atrocities, famine warnings, and displacement spread as ceasefire attempts repeatedly collapse.
Kiir and Machar sign a power-sharing peace agreement mediated by IGAD, though implementation falters.
Renewed fighting in Juba shatters the peace deal, forcing Riek Machar to flee the country.
The UN declares famine in parts of South Sudan, the first formally declared famine anywhere in years, amid ongoing conflict.
Kiir and Machar sign the Revitalized Agreement on the Resolution of the Conflict, establishing a renewed roadmap to peace.
A transitional unity government forms with Riek Machar returning as first vice president, easing the civil war.
The transitional period is extended by two years as the formation of unified armed forces and a permanent constitution stalls.
Conflict in neighboring Sudan sends large numbers of refugees and returnees into South Sudan, straining its fragile economy.
Long-delayed national elections are postponed again to 2026 as the transitional government extends its mandate.
Renewed tensions and the detention of First Vice President Riek Machar threaten the fragile 2018 peace agreement.
